title: Encoders, Decoders, and Multiplexers
summary: Decoders, encoders, multiplexers, demultiplexers, and their applications in digital systems.
content_html:
Encoders, Decoders, and Multiplexers

Beyond basic gates and adders, digital systems require specialized combinational circuits for routing data, selecting between multiple sources, and converting between different code formats. Decoders, encoders, multiplexers, and demultiplexers are the building blocks for memory addressing, data buses, and control logic in computers.

Decoder

A decoder takes n binary input lines and activates exactly one of its 2n output lines — the one corresponding to the binary value on the inputs. At any given time, exactly one output is HIGH and all others are LOW.

A 2-to-4 decoder has 2 inputs (A, B) and 4 outputs (Y0-Y3):

  • A=0, B=0 → Y0=1 (others 0)
  • A=0, B=1 → Y1=1 (others 0)
  • A=1, B=0 → Y2=1 (others 0)
  • A=1, B=1 → Y3=1 (others 0)

Decoders are used extensively in memory systems: an address decoder takes a memory address and activates only the specific memory chip or row that corresponds to that address. A 3-to-8 decoder is common in such applications.

An enable input is often added to a decoder. When enable is LOW, all outputs are forced LOW regardless of inputs, allowing multiple decoders to be chained for larger address spaces.

Encoder

An encoder is the inverse of a decoder. It takes 2n input lines (exactly one should be HIGH) and produces the n-bit binary code corresponding to which input is active.

For example, a 4-to-2 encoder with input I2 HIGH outputs the binary code 10 (decimal 2).

The priority encoder is a more practical variant. When multiple inputs are simultaneously HIGH, the priority encoder outputs the binary address of the highest-priority active input (typically the highest-numbered input). It also includes a valid output that indicates whether any input is active at all. Priority encoders are used in interrupt controllers: when multiple hardware devices request service simultaneously, the CPU must determine which has highest priority.

Multiplexer (MUX)

A multiplexer (MUX) is a data selector: it routes one of 2n data inputs to a single output, based on the value of n select lines.

A 4-to-1 MUX has 4 data inputs (D0-D3), 2 select lines (S1, S0), and 1 output:

  • S1=0, S0=0 → Output = D0
  • S1=0, S0=1 → Output = D1
  • S1=1, S0=0 → Output = D2
  • S1=1, S0=1 → Output = D3

The Boolean expression for a 4-to-1 MUX output is: Y = S1’S0’D0 + S1’S0D1 + S1S0’D2 + S1S0D3.

Multiplexers are used for data routing (selecting which source feeds the ALU), time-division multiplexing (sharing a communication channel among multiple signals), and even implementing arbitrary Boolean functions (a 2n-to-1 MUX can implement any n-variable Boolean function).

Demultiplexer (DEMUX)

A demultiplexer is the inverse of a multiplexer. It routes a single input to one of 2n outputs, based on n select lines. A 1-to-4 DEMUX with 2 select lines sends its input to one of 4 output channels.

Interestingly, a decoder with an enable input can function as a demultiplexer: the enable acts as the data input, and the address lines serve as select lines.

Applications in Computer Systems

These components appear throughout computer architecture:

  • Memory address decoding: A decoder activates the correct memory bank or chip for a given address.
  • Bus multiplexing: A MUX allows multiple devices to share a single data bus, with one device selected at a time.
  • Instruction decoding: The control unit uses decoder-like logic to activate the correct datapath based on the opcode of the current instruction.
  • Function generators: MUXes can implement arbitrary Boolean functions directly, useful in FPGAs (Field-Programmable Gate Arrays).
  • Keyboard encoders: A priority encoder converts a key press (one of many inputs) into a binary code representing which key was pressed.

Building Larger Circuits from Smaller Ones

One of the key principles of digital design is hierarchical composition: small circuits like decoders and MUXes are used as building blocks for larger systems. A 3-to-8 decoder can be built from two 2-to-4 decoders and some additional logic. A 16-to-1 MUX can be built from five 4-to-1 MUXes. This modular approach makes designing, testing, and verifying complex systems manageable.

exercises.title: Decoder Output Count
exercises.prompt: How many outputs does a 3-to-8 decoder have?

exercises.title: MUX Select Lines
exercises.prompt: How many select lines does a 4-to-1 multiplexer require?

exercises.title: Priority Encoder Use Case
exercises.prompt: Where is a priority encoder most commonly used in a computer system?

exercises.title: Decoder Active Outputs
exercises.prompt: For a 2-to-4 decoder with inputs A=1, B=0, how many outputs are HIGH?
